0: Environmental impacts of a dairy cheese chain including whey feeding: An Italian case study
Nadia Palmieri a, , Maria Bonaventura Forleo Elisabetta Salimei b *

a Department of Economics, Management, Society and Institutions, University of Molise, Via F. De Sanctis, 86100 Campobasso, Italy b Department of Agriculture, Environment and Food Sciences, University of Molise, Via F. De Sanctis, 86100 Campobasso, Italy

A R T E N|ABSTRACT Article history: Received 14 November 2015 Received in revised form 28 June 2016 Accepted 29 June 2016 Available online 4 July 2016|The dairy industry is associated with the production of wastewaters and effluents that could have a significant environmental impact because of their pollutant characteristics. Cheese whey is an important source of pollution that needs an appropriate treatment because of several environmental burdens. Thanks to its nutritional value, liquid whey can be successfully recycled in animal nutrition. Following the LCA methodology, this study aims to contribute to the literature on the environmental sustainability of cheese production in a traditional dairy chain, based on an Italian case study and on liquid whey usage. The environmental assessment considers both the milk and the mozzarella cheese production. Three dairy chains and cow diets were assessed and compared: the A diet, with traditional feeding hay and no liquid whey; the B diet, with silages but no liquid whey; the C diet, including both silages and liquid whey. A physical allocation method was used to share the environmental burden between milk and meat at farm level, while the environmental burden of the mozzarella cheese production was totally allocated to curd. A sensitivity analysis based on the cereal unit allocation method was performed. A further sensitivity analysis was performed to compare what the effect would have been considering the vari- ability of milk yield observed in the three diets. Results showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain with liquid whey recycling did not result the lowest impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, showing best performances on some impacts, but not on others. However, sensitivity analysis testing for the observed yield variability showed that the C diet with whey usage could represent the best option among the different diets. © 2016 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
1: Humanity consumes more resources than our planet is able to regenerate (Sala et al., 2013). Several studies have showed that many food supply chains are not sustainable because of the envi- ronmental impacts occurring in different phases along the chains (Notarnicola et al., 2015). The food and beverages are responsible for 20-30% of the environmental impacts caused by private con- sumption in Europe (Fantin et al., 2012). Even if dairy products constitute a significant source of daily nutrients for human con- sumption (Haug et al., 2007; Weaver, 2010) and are highly rec- ommended as part of a healthy and balanced diet (Kim et al., 2014),

they have a great environmental impact (Tukker et al., 2006), especially in terms of resource depletion and greenhouse gas emissions (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a). Moreover, the dairy in- dustry is usually considered responsible for the production of wastewaters and effluents that could have a significant environ- mental impact because of their pollutant characteristics (Mirabella et al., 2014; Prazeres et al., 2012). The dairy sector makes several products, manufactured in very different production lines, but always originating from the same raw material, i.e. the raw milk. Some studies calculated the envi- ronmental impact of different products and concluded that the milk production was the most impactful phase, followed by cheesemaking production (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Milk production is the most critical phase along the dairy chain, with contributions ranging from 63% to 89% on all environmental impact categories according to Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a), and

between 81% and 97% of the global warming potential (GWP) ac- cording to Finnegan et al. (2015). According to Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a; 2013b), milk production shows the highest impact on acidification (AC), eutrophication (EU) and global warning po- tential (GWP) categories mainly due to manure management, including its application to soil (on AC and EU categories), and to enteric fermentation (on GWP category). According to some Au- thors (Cederberg and Mattsson, 2000; Roma et al., 2015), the environmental impact of milk production depends on the different feeding strategies used.

The cheese-making has the highest environmental impacts on abiotic depletion, ozone layer depletion and cumulative energy demand categories, mainly due to the energy needs required in different production processes (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013b). Both cheese-making and packaging phases contributed each about 7%- 13% to energy use and about 3%-4% to GWP (van Middelaar et al., 2011). According to Kim et al. (2013), the carbon footprint of mozzarella cheese was 7.28 kg CO2e/kg mozzarella cheese, and the carbon footprint of one kg of dry whey was 12.1 kg CO2e/kg whey delivered for other purposes, such as animal feed. In a subsequent study of Kim et al. (2014), the carbon footprint of mozzarella cheese was 9.30 kg CO2e/kg.

Cheese whey is the main pollutant generated from the cheese production (Rajeshwari et al., 2000). Whey can cause several environmental impacts (Prazeres et al., 2012; Rivas et al., 2011) and cannot be directly discharged to the environment without an appropriate treatment. The proper industrial use or disposal treatment of the cheese whey depends on its quality and quantity, on the available technologies, on the costs associated with the treatment, and on the local disposal standards imposed by envi- ronmental legislations (Prazeres et al., 2012). The polluting power of whey has led countries such as United States, Canada, Australia, New Zealand, and the European Union, to introduce strict envi- ronment protection legislation (Smithers, 2008). Such a legislative framework against improper disposal of whey and in favour of its recycling-encouraged the dairy industry to explore other ap- proaches and opportunities for the management of dairy effluents (Smithers, 2008).
Thanks to its high nutritional value, liquid whey can be recycled within the dairy chain for feeding animals (EWPA, 2006).

In the Italian agro-food sector, the cheese production is highly important at global (in 2013 it was 1.16 X 106 t; source: Istat, 2013) and local levels, and presents a variety of traditional and typical products, of artisanal and industrial processes. It is worth noting that approximately 1 kg cheese produces 10 L cheese whey (Lustrato et al., 2013).

Within this context, the study aims to contribute to the litera- ture on the environmental sustainability of mozzarella cheese production in a traditional dairy chain, based on an Italian case study dealing with liquid whey recycling. Following the LCA methodology, impacts of mozzarella cheese production were assessed and different animal diets were compared. The analysis of the main environmental impacts is useful in order to assess, to compare and to suggest some modifications in the dairy chains and in cow diets.

7: The main purpose of the study was to assess the environmental impact of mozzarella production based on milk produced following different feeding strategies at farm level, and including or not whey usage.
The environmental impact assessment has been carried out by

considering a dairy farm and a cheese factory located in Molise region, the second smallest region located in the South Centre of Italy, where the cheese production has a strong traditional char- acter (Molise Region, PSR, 2014-2010). Despite the small size of the region, the local cheese production contributes approximately to 1.8% of the national cheese production (Istat, 2013).

The environmental impact analysis was carried out through the LCA methodology (ISO 14040, 2006; ISO 14044, 2006) that repre- sents a useful tool, both to identify and to investigate impacts within the whole supply chain (Sala and Castellani, 2009).
8: The functional unit (FU) was 123 of mozzarella cheese made from 1 L of cow milk.

The LCA system boundary (Fig. refers to the first two phases of a dairy supply chain, namely the dairy farming and the cheese- making phases. The boundary considers both the whole life cycle of cows inside the dairy farm-including the agricultural processes of feedstuffs-, and the cheese factory -including all the activities that take place for the mozzarella cheese-making, from the milk reception to the mozzarella production and the liquid whey disposal-.
Three dairy chains were assessed, called from A to C, whose differences are described below. At dairy farm level we considered three diets, called from A to C, with different ration composition.

Moreover, the A-C diets differ in the usage/non usage of liquid whey. In our study, the whole amount of liquid cheese whey ob- tained as by-product of the mozzarella cheese-making may have two alternative fates, i.e. it is delivered to the wastewater treatment outside the cheese factory or it is recycled for feeding animals of the case farm which is located close to the cheese factory. In two chains (A and B chains), the whole amount of liquid cheese whey is mixed with the wastewater effluent from the mill in a municipal waste- water treatment plant (WWTP). In the third chain, the C one, the whole amount of liquid whey produced at cheese-making level was delivered to the case farm and used, after microbial stabilization, in animal feeding as partial substitute of drinking water (C diet).

As baseline scenario, the physical allocation method was used to share the environmental burden between milk and meat at farm level, while the environmental burden of the mozzarella produc- tion was totally allocated to curd (Gonzales-Garcia et al., 2013a). The percentages of physical allocation at case farm level were 88% to milk and 12% to meat (as live weight cow and calf) (IDF, 2015). The manure/slurry allocation was not necessary because manure was used as a fertilizer in the feed cultivation.
9: The functional unit (FU) was 123 of mozzarella cheese made from 1 L of cow milk.

The LCA system boundary (Fig. refers to the first two phases of a dairy supply chain, namely the dairy farming and the cheese- making phases. The boundary considers both the whole life cycle of cows inside the dairy farm-including the agricultural processes of feedstuffs-, and the cheese factory -including all the activities that take place for the mozzarella cheese-making, from the milk reception to the mozzarella production and the liquid whey disposal-.
Three dairy chains were assessed, called from A to C, whose differences are described below. At dairy farm level we considered three diets, called from A to C, with different ration composition.

Moreover, the A-C diets differ in the usage/non usage of liquid whey. In our study, the whole amount of liquid cheese whey ob- tained as by-product of the mozzarella cheese-making may have two alternative fates, i.e. it is delivered to the wastewater treatment outside the cheese factory or it is recycled for feeding animals of the case farm which is located close to the cheese factory. In two chains (A and B chains), the whole amount of liquid cheese whey is mixed with the wastewater effluent from the mill in a municipal waste- water treatment plant (WWTP). In the third chain, the C one, the whole amount of liquid whey produced at cheese-making level was delivered to the case farm and used, after microbial stabilization, in animal feeding as partial substitute of drinking water (C diet).

As baseline scenario, the physical allocation method was used to share the environmental burden between milk and meat at farm level, while the environmental burden of the mozzarella produc- tion was totally allocated to curd (Gonzales-Garcia et al., 2013a). The percentages of physical allocation at case farm level were 88% to milk and 12% to meat (as live weight cow and calf) (IDF, 2015). The manure/slurry allocation was not necessary because manure was used as a fertilizer in the feed cultivation.
10: The functional unit (FU) was 123 of mozzarella cheese made from 1 L of cow milk.

11: Data for the life cycle inventory analysis were collected through a questionnaire drawn up according to the guidelines for the application of LCA to food and agricultural products (Neri, 2009), through databases (Ecoinvent database V. 3.0; http://www. recoinvent.org/database/database.html) and some literature (Cecchini et al., 2004; Falconi et al., 2005; Guerra, 2007; Kim et al., 2013).

Primary data (Tables 1-3) were collected from two firms (a dairy farm and a cheese factory) located in Molise region, which followed an on field experiment about different feeding strategies for dairy cows whose milk production was processed into mozza- rella cheese. The data are referred to 2015.

Primary data from the case farm were about the milk quantity and quality, the Italian Friesian cow rations and water consump- tion, and about the manure/slurry produced. The case farm experimented different diets that are nutritionally similar: diet A,

Data reported in Table 1 is related to the management of animals in the case farm where lactating animals were divided into three groups of 18 cows each. The three groups were homogeneous and com- parable in terms of milk yield, days of lactation and parity. Indi- vidual milk production and nutritional status of animals in the experimental groups were monitored and recorded for 80 days. The observed productive results are consistent with values reported in literature (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Besides no effects on milk production, liquid whey in balanced lactating cow diets does not affect body weight of animals, reproduction performances, mammary health or rumen activity parameters; however it is reported as an interesting diet

Tendencies to differences in milk quality, i.e. fat and protein contents, among diets were observed at 0.05 < P < 0.1 level, ac- cording to literature (Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Based on the average productive results, fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) has been calculated on a 305 days basis for each experimental group (Table 1) and used in the present LCA study. Table 2 shows the composition of the diets; on this regard it is worth noting that feedstuffs were offered as total mixed rations, with the exception of liquid cheese whey microbi- ologically stabilized offered to C cows as partial substitute of drinking water. For this reason, the water consumption in C diet was lower than the one in A and B diets.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

The choice of the allocation procedure may affect the results of LCA study. Different allocation approaches for co-product are one of the main causes of uncertainty in LCA results of agricultural sector (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014; Flysjo et al., 2012).

For this reason, a first sensitivity analysis was performed changing the allocation method according to a cereal unit (CU) method for allocating milk production and for comparing the impact of the dairy chains.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

In C cow diet the whole amount of liquid cheese whey, microbiolog- ically stabilized, was used in animal feeding as partial substitute of drinking water (in C heifer diet, 28% of water was substituted with whey; in C lactating cow diet, 44% of water was substituted). Data reported in Table 1 is related to the management of animals in the case farm where lactating animals were divided into three groups of 18 cows each. The three groups were homogeneous and com- parable in terms of milk yield, days of lactation and parity. Indi- vidual milk production and nutritional status of animals in the experimental groups were monitored and recorded for 80 days. The value of annual milk production was extrapolated based on the values monitored over 80 days, since the turnover of lactating COWS is constant during the year. Raw data underwent analysis of vari- ance also considering the covariate effects of length of lactation and pregnancy. Corrected mean values of daily milk yield were not statistically different (diet A: 26.31(+1.4, sem) L/d; diet B: 26.45 (+1.2, sem) L/d; and diet C: 25.78 (+0.7, sem) L/d). The observed productive results are consistent with values reported in literature (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Besides no effects on milk production, liquid whey in balanced lactating cow diets does not affect body weight of animals, reproduction performances, mammary health or rumen activity parameters; however it is reported as an interesting diet

ingredient from an economical point of view, when distances from the cheese industry are short and costs of handling and trans- portation are high (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Succi et al., 1986).

Tendencies to differences in milk quality, i.e. fat and protein contents, among diets were observed at 0.05 < P < 0.1 level, ac- cording to literature (Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Based on the average productive results, fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) has been calculated on a 305 days basis for each experimental group (Table 1) and used in the present LCA study. Table 2 shows the composition of the diets; on this regard it is worth noting that feedstuffs were offered as total mixed rations, with the exception of liquid cheese whey microbi- ologically stabilized offered to C cows as partial substitute of drinking water. For this reason, the water consumption in C diet was lower than the one in A and B diets.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

plant that follows a three stage treatment (mechanical, biological, chemical) including sludge digestion (Ecoinvent database).
2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

The choice of the allocation procedure may affect the results of LCA study. Different allocation approaches for co-product are one of the main causes of uncertainty in LCA results of agricultural sector (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014; Flysjo et al., 2012).

For this reason, a first sensitivity analysis was performed changing the allocation method according to a cereal unit (CU) method for allocating milk production and for comparing the impact of the dairy chains.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

The choice of the allocation procedure may affect the results of LCA study. Different allocation approaches for co-product are one of the main causes of uncertainty in LCA results of agricultural sector (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014; Flysjo et al., 2012).

For this reason, a first sensitivity analysis was performed changing the allocation method according to a cereal unit (CU) method for allocating milk production and for comparing the impact of the dairy chains.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

with traditional feeding hay and no liquid whey; diet B, with silages but no liquid whey; diet C including both silages and liquid whey. In C cow diet the whole amount of liquid cheese whey, microbiolog- ically stabilized, was used in animal feeding as partial substitute of drinking water (in C heifer diet, 28% of water was substituted with whey; in C lactating cow diet, 44% of water was substituted). Data reported in Table 1 is related to the management of animals in the case farm where lactating animals were divided into three groups of 18 cows each. The three groups were homogeneous and com- parable in terms of milk yield, days of lactation and parity. Indi- vidual milk production and nutritional status of animals in the experimental groups were monitored and recorded for 80 days. The value of annual milk production was extrapolated based on the values monitored over 80 days, since the turnover of lactating COWS is constant during the year. Raw data underwent analysis of vari- ance also considering the covariate effects of length of lactation and pregnancy. Corrected mean values of daily milk yield were not statistically different (diet A: 26.31(+1.4, sem) L/d; diet B: 26.45 (+1.2, sem) L/d; and diet C: 25.78 (+0.7, sem) L/d). The observed productive results are consistent with values reported in literature (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Besides no effects on milk production, liquid whey in balanced lactating cow diets does not affect body weight of animals, reproduction performances, mammary health or rumen activity parameters; however it is reported as an interesting diet

kg of mozzarella produced by 1 L of milk|0.123 kg of whey produced by 0.123 kg of mozzarella|0.11 Fat in mozzarella (g/100 g of product)|18.50 Protein in mozzarella (g/100 g of product)|15.40 Fat in whey (g/100 g of product)|0.2 Protein in whey (g/100 g of product)|0.7 Resources consumption|Resources consumption Electricity consumption (kWh/0.123 kg of mozzarella)|0.025 Heat consumption (MJ/0.123 kg of mozzarella)|0.014 Water consumption (L/0.123 kg of mozzarella)|2,23

ingredient from an economical point of view, when distances from the cheese industry are short and costs of handling and trans- portation are high (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Succi et al., 1986).

Tendencies to differences in milk quality, i.e. fat and protein contents, among diets were observed at 0.05 < P < 0.1 level, ac- cording to literature (Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Based on the average productive results, fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) has been calculated on a 305 days basis for each experimental group (Table 1) and used in the present LCA study. Table 2 shows the composition of the diets; on this regard it is worth noting that feedstuffs were offered as total mixed rations, with the exception of liquid cheese whey microbi- ologically stabilized offered to C cows as partial substitute of drinking water. For this reason, the water consumption in C diet was lower than the one in A and B diets.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

plant that follows a three stage treatment (mechanical, biological, chemical) including sludge digestion (Ecoinvent database).
2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

The choice of the allocation procedure may affect the results of LCA study. Different allocation approaches for co-product are one of the main causes of uncertainty in LCA results of agricultural sector (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014; Flysjo et al., 2012).

For this reason, a first sensitivity analysis was performed changing the allocation method according to a cereal unit (CU) method for allocating milk production and for comparing the impact of the dairy chains.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

ingredient from an economical point of view, when distances from the cheese industry are short and costs of handling and trans- portation are high (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Succi et al., 1986).

Tendencies to differences in milk quality, i.e. fat and protein contents, among diets were observed at 0.05 < P < 0.1 level, ac- cording to literature (Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Based on the average productive results, fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) has been calculated on a 305 days basis for each experimental group (Table 1) and used in the present LCA study. Table 2 shows the composition of the diets; on this regard it is worth noting that feedstuffs were offered as total mixed rations, with the exception of liquid cheese whey microbi- ologically stabilized offered to C cows as partial substitute of drinking water. For this reason, the water consumption in C diet was lower than the one in A and B diets.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

The choice of the allocation procedure may affect the results of LCA study. Different allocation approaches for co-product are one of the main causes of uncertainty in LCA results of agricultural sector (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014; Flysjo et al., 2012).

For this reason, a first sensitivity analysis was performed changing the allocation method according to a cereal unit (CU) method for allocating milk production and for comparing the impact of the dairy chains.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

ingredient from an economical point of view, when distances from the cheese industry are short and costs of handling and trans- portation are high (Anderson et al., 1974; Pinchasov et al., 1982; Succi et al., 1986).

Tendencies to differences in milk quality, i.e. fat and protein contents, among diets were observed at 0.05 < P < 0.1 level, ac- cording to literature (Pinchasov et al., 1982; Schingoethe, 1976; Succi et al., 1986). Based on the average productive results, fat and protein corrected milk (FPCM) has been calculated on a 305 days basis for each experimental group (Table 1) and used in the present LCA study. Table 2 shows the composition of the diets; on this regard it is worth noting that feedstuffs were offered as total mixed rations, with the exception of liquid cheese whey microbi- ologically stabilized offered to C cows as partial substitute of drinking water. For this reason, the water consumption in C diet was lower than the one in A and B diets.

Primary data from the cheese factory concern the mozzarella production per day and per milk processed, the percentages of fat and protein in mozzarella cheese and in whey, the electricity con- sumption, the water and heat consumption. The cheese factory is a traditional cheese factory that produced 1620 kg of mozzarella/day. Data from the cheese factory have been recorded throughout the experiment and summarised in Table 3.
When it was not possible to get real data, inventory data were collected from literature and Ecoinvent database (v. 3.0) as described below (Table 4).

The enteric and animal housing emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated, for the Italian context, the CH4 emissions and the ammonia emissions quantified according to the international guidelines from EMEP/EEA (2009). Nitrous oxide (N20) emissions from animal housing that do not use beddings (as in our case) are very low and were not considered according to Battini et al. (2016).

The storage emissions of methane (CH4) and nitrous oxide (N20) came from Dalla Riva et al. (2014) study that calculated these emissions in the Italian context using IPCC (2006) (Tier 2) and Ispra (2008) methods. Ammonia (NH3) emissions came from Falconi et al. (2011) that estimated, in the Italian context, emissions due to manure/slurry storage using Ispra (2008) method. Moreover, nitrogen oxides (NOx) emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study that estimated these emissions using the factor recom- mended by IPCC (2006).

Emissions related to manure/slurry spreading (N20, NH3, NOx) and nitrate leaching were taken from Battini et al. (2016) study that calculated them according to IPCC (2006). The P leaching run-off emissions came from Battini et al. (2016) study, while emission factor of Potassium, Copper and Zinc were taken from Falconi et al. (2005).

Data referred to feed cultivation came from Ecoinvent database. The inventory for each feed cultivation included the processes of soil cultivation, sowing, weed control, fertilisation, pest and pathogen control, harvest and grain drying. The production of commercial mixed feeds was from the Ecoinvent database and inventory included the processing of feedstuffs (crushing or milling, heat treatment, dosing, mixing and pelleting) and the storage of the mixed feeds.

The inventory for each step of mozzarella production included machineries/tools and products used (i.e. nitric acid and sodium hydroxide, disinfectants); data were taken from the Ecoinvent database. Primary data for electricity, heat and water consumptions were also considered.

Data referred to trucks used for transporting the raw milk came from Ecoinvent database. Raw milk was transported from dairy farm to the cheese factory in diesel trucks of 16-t capacity for an average distance of 10 km (based on the real location of the farm and the factory).

Finally, data referred to the wastewater treatment plant for whey disposal, including whey transport from the cheese factory to the treatment plant came from Ecoinvent database. Wastewaters are purified in a moderately large municipal wastewater treatment

2.4. Sensitivity analysis: allocation method and variability of milk yield
The objective of a sensitivity analysis is to test parameters that can strongly influence the final output of the LCA assessment.

This sensitivity analysis involved the case farm level because many studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaaret al., 2011) showed that milk production is more impactful than cheese-making. The CU alloca- tion method is based on a common denominator, i.e. metabolizable energy content of product and co-product for feed purpose, that allows to consider agricultural products and co-products used in different sectors (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014). This method applies to mass proportions of products and co-products a specific conversion factor in terms of CU (i.e. 1 kg milk = 0.8 CU; 1 kg live- weight dairy cow = 6.30 CU; 1 kg live-weight fattening calf = 2.69 CU). The CU method generates percentage of allocation between the approaches of mass, energy and economic allocation, so reducing the sensitivity of LCA results in agricultural sector to the allocation choices. The environmental burden was allocated 86.6% to milk, 6.8% to live-weight dairy cow and 6.6% to live-weight fattening calf (Brankatschk and Finkbeiner, 2014).
A further sensitivity analysis, was performed to compare the impact of the dairy chains while considering the observed vari- ability of milk yield.

14: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. This method considers the following environmental impacts: abiotic depletion (AD), abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (ADf), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), human toxicity (HT), fresh water and marine aquatic eco-toxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial eco-toxicity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), acidifi- cation (AC), and eutrophication (EU). Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

Results on the environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella cheese showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain, irrespective of the diet followed at the case farm level (Diet A, Fig. 2; Diet B, Fig. 3; Diet C, Fig. 4).

This high contribution of milk production phase to the envi- ronmental impact of the dairy chain is in agreement with other studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
Mozzarella production had some impacts on HT, ADf, AD, ME and ODP impacts. The impacts on HT, AD and ME categories were due to the Chromium (on HT and AD categories) and to Nickel (on

Fig. 2. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet A) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet B) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet C) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the case farm.

ME category) from machinery and tools used in milk reception and pasteurization steps. Moreover, cheese factory (i.e. mozzarella production process) had significant thermal energy consumption obtained from the combustion of fossil fuels to produce steam and hot water used in the pasteurisation step. The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a).

Impacts of transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant or from factory to the dairy farm-seemed not very important and this is consistent with the short distance between the locations of the firms involved -the farm and the factory-.

The relative burden of the wastewater treatment (in A and B diet) along the whole dairy chain was negligible and involved mainly the two categories of marine ecotoxicity and eutrophica- tion. Even in this case, our findings appear in line with Gonzalez- Garcia et al. (2013a).

The characterization results (Fig. 5), based on a cradle-to- processing-gate boundary, showed that dairy supply chain in case of A diet had the worst environmental performance on many impact categories. The worst performances of this chain compared

Fig. 5. Results of the characterization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (phys- ical allocation). For each category, the percentage expresses the relative impact of each chain respect to the most impactful dairy supply chain (100%).

to others are on AD, ME, ODP, HT and FW impacts. Even on those impacts where the A chain was not the worst option, lower per- formances than B chain were observed. The A diet performances are mainly related to agricultural processes for hay production, to the wastewater whey disposal, finally, to the milk yield.

On GWP, PO, AC and EU impact categories the differences among the three chains were not so strong, but the dairy chain C appeared the worst solution as mainly related to manure/slurry production in liquid whey recycling diet. On the other side, the C chain was preferable for its lowest impact on AD, ODP and ADf categories, because of the whey usage instead of wastewater disposal, because of agricultural processes for silage production, finally due to milk yield.
The dairy chain with B diet resulted the lowest impacting chain for several categories, even if quite similar impacts to that of the C chain emerged in many cases.

These findings were confirmed when looking at the normali- zation figure (Fig. 6). Even if normalization only scales some impact categories up or down, the magnitude of results was reported for impact categories and each dairy chains. Four impact categories emerged for their magnitude, i.e. acidification, eutrophication, marine ecotoxicity and global warming potential. The impact of A diet was really high compared to the other diets on marine eco- toxicity and, even if to a low degree, also on abiotic and ozone depletion.

The impacts on GWP and PO categories (Table 5) were mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation, confirming current literature (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b). The burden on AC category was mainly due to ammonia emissions from the volatilization of the nitrogen contained in manure, in agreement with Berlin (2002) study.

With regard to the EU impact, the main eutrophying emissions that contribute to the EU category were the ammonia emissions and the leaching of nitrate derived from the manure application on the field (Table these results were in agreement with Battini et al. (2016) and Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013b) studies.

Impacts on AD category were due to Copper (chain A), Lead (chain B) and Chromium (chain C) coming from agricultural pro- cesses for fodder production, which are different among chains, and milk reception and pasteurization steps.

The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a). Moreover, the transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant in A and B chains, or from factory to the dairy farm in C chain-, notwithstanding the short distance between the locations involved, impacted on AD, ADf, and ODP categories.
The impact on ME was due to Beryllium coming from the disposal treatment of the cheese whey in WWTP (A and B chains). Moreover, Copper and Zinc due to manure/slurry spreading caused
Fig. 6. Results of the normalization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (physical allocation).

Impact categories|Dairy chains|Substances|Processes AD|A|Copper|Agricultural processes for fodder production, milk reception and pasteurization steps |B|Lead| |C|Chromium| ADf|A, B. C|Oil crude|Pasteurisation GWP|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation ODP|A, B. C|Methane bromotrifluoro, Halon 1301|Oil combustion and extraction during pasteurization step HT|A, B. C|Chromium|Milk reception and pasteurization steps FW|A, B. C|Copper|Manure/slurry spreading ME|A, B|Beryllium|WWTP TE|A, B. C|Zinc and Copper|Manure/slurry spreading PO|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation AC|A, B. C|Ammonia|Manure/slurry storage and spreading EU|A, B. C|Ammonia and nitrate|Manure/slurry storage and spreading

impacts on FW and TE categories. According to these findings, as expected, we cannot find a dairy chain to be considered the best option under a global perspective across all impact categories. Moreover and against our hypothesis, this is not the case for the chain in which the liquid whey is internally used in cow diets (C chain). We explain this result in the light of the environmental burden of the milk production phase that enter in each chains, irrespective of the followed diet, notwithstanding the average milk yield in group C was not statis- tically lower than those in groups A and B. On this regard, the strategy of grouping cows according to their lactation stage (i.e. beginning and late lactation), and of feeding them different diets (i.e. considering different voluntary feed intake and nutrient re- quirements), usually adopted in large dairy farms, should be tested also in small dairy farms like the one here considered. This strategy could be useful in increasing milk yield, improving the health status of animals while getting possible positive environmental impacts of the primary production in the traditional dairy chain.

Taken together, results obtained in this study were in line with other LCA studies where different dairy products were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Despite different environmental assessment methods used in literature, the milk production, i.e. the main raw material in the mozzarella production, is the process which mostly contributed to the environmental impact in all the categories. In particular, the most impactful categories, coming from normalization results, were the AC, EC and GWP impacts (see Fig. 6). Berlin (2002) showed that the raw milk production is by far the largest contributor to AC, EU, GWP and PO. The main hotspots for AC, EC and GWP environmental impacts were pointed out by Hospido et al. (2003) and Fantin et al. (2012). Hogaas Eide (2002) showed that the dairy farm was the main factor in terms of EC, AC (due to the leakage of nutrients and ammonia losses from manure application) and GWP (caused by the methane emissions from the cattle). Methane from enteric fermentation was the pre- dominant emission source also in Kim et al. (2013) study. Moreover, in Kim et al. (2013) the Climate Change was also impacted by the manure management that caused environmental burden on both GWP and EU categories. Even in van Middelaar et al. (2011) study, the enteric fermentation was the main emission source impacting on GWP category.
Our results and current literature showed that GWP, PO, AC and EU are important categories on which is worth focusing improve- ment alternatives.
The proposal of improvement alternatives at dairy-farm level should be interesting from an environmental point of view in the whole dairy supply chain.

According to Patra (2012), the emission of enteric fermentation might be influenced by the proportion of forages and by the quality of forages in the ration. If it is true that methane emission from the rumen activity is positively related to the portion of roughage in the diet (Kristensen et al., 2011), the negative effect on ruminal health of an excess of non structural carbohydrates (starch and sugars) and the related risk of acidosis should be also considered. Moreover, according to Thomassen et al. (2008), the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the animal slow the rate of fermentation in the rumen. Hospido et al. (2003) and Castanheira et al. (2010) proposed alternatives focused on changes in the dairy ration and on the manure management. According to Kristensen et al. (2015) different types of hay in cow ration do not lead to a significant reduction of GHG emissions.

Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.
15: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. This method considers the following environmental impacts: abiotic depletion (AD), abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (ADf), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), human toxicity (HT), fresh water and marine aquatic eco-toxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial eco-toxicity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), acidifi- cation (AC), and eutrophication (EU). Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

Results on the environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella cheese showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain, irrespective of the diet followed at the case farm level (Diet A, Fig. 2; Diet B, Fig. 3; Diet C, Fig. 4).

This high contribution of milk production phase to the envi- ronmental impact of the dairy chain is in agreement with other studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
Mozzarella production had some impacts on HT, ADf, AD, ME and ODP impacts. The impacts on HT, AD and ME categories were due to the Chromium (on HT and AD categories) and to Nickel (on

ME category) from machinery and tools used in milk reception and pasteurization steps. Moreover, cheese factory (i.e. mozzarella production process) had significant thermal energy consumption obtained from the combustion of fossil fuels to produce steam and hot water used in the pasteurisation step. The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a).

Impacts of transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant or from factory to the dairy farm-seemed not very important and this is consistent with the short distance between the locations of the firms involved -the farm and the factory-.

The relative burden of the wastewater treatment (in A and B diet) along the whole dairy chain was negligible and involved mainly the two categories of marine ecotoxicity and eutrophica- tion. Even in this case, our findings appear in line with Gonzalez- Garcia et al. (2013a).

The characterization results (Fig. 5), based on a cradle-to- processing-gate boundary, showed that dairy supply chain in case of A diet had the worst environmental performance on many impact categories. The worst performances of this chain compared

Fig. 5. Results of the characterization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (phys- ical allocation). For each category, the percentage expresses the relative impact of each chain respect to the most impactful dairy supply chain (100%).

to others are on AD, ME, ODP, HT and FW impacts. Even on those impacts where the A chain was not the worst option, lower per- formances than B chain were observed. The A diet performances are mainly related to agricultural processes for hay production, to the wastewater whey disposal, finally, to the milk yield.

On GWP, PO, AC and EU impact categories the differences among the three chains were not so strong, but the dairy chain C appeared the worst solution as mainly related to manure/slurry production in liquid whey recycling diet. On the other side, the C chain was preferable for its lowest impact on AD, ODP and ADf categories, because of the whey usage instead of wastewater disposal, because of agricultural processes for silage production, finally due to milk yield.
The dairy chain with B diet resulted the lowest impacting chain for several categories, even if quite similar impacts to that of the C chain emerged in many cases.

These findings were confirmed when looking at the normali- zation figure (Fig. 6). Even if normalization only scales some impact categories up or down, the magnitude of results was reported for impact categories and each dairy chains. Four impact categories emerged for their magnitude, i.e. acidification, eutrophication, marine ecotoxicity and global warming potential. The impact of A diet was really high compared to the other diets on marine eco- toxicity and, even if to a low degree, also on abiotic and ozone depletion.

The impacts on GWP and PO categories (Table 5) were mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation, confirming current literature (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b). The burden on AC category was mainly due to ammonia emissions from the volatilization of the nitrogen contained in manure, in agreement with Berlin (2002) study.

With regard to the EU impact, the main eutrophying emissions that contribute to the EU category were the ammonia emissions and the leaching of nitrate derived from the manure application on the field (Table these results were in agreement with Battini et al. (2016) and Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013b) studies.

Impacts on AD category were due to Copper (chain A), Lead (chain B) and Chromium (chain C) coming from agricultural pro- cesses for fodder production, which are different among chains, and milk reception and pasteurization steps.

The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a). Moreover, the transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant in A and B chains, or from factory to the dairy farm in C chain-, notwithstanding the short distance between the locations involved, impacted on AD, ADf, and ODP categories.
The impact on ME was due to Beryllium coming from the disposal treatment of the cheese whey in WWTP (A and B chains). Moreover, Copper and Zinc due to manure/slurry spreading caused

C|Oil crude|Pasteurisation GWP|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation ODP|A, B. C|Methane bromotrifluoro, Halon 1301|Oil combustion and extraction during pasteurization step HT|A, B. C|Chromium|Milk reception and pasteurization steps FW|A, B. C|Copper|Manure/slurry spreading ME|A, B|Beryllium|WWTP TE|A, B. C|Zinc and Copper|Manure/slurry spreading PO|A, B. impacts on FW and TE categories. According to these findings, as expected, we cannot find a dairy chain to be considered the best option under a global perspective across all impact categories. Moreover and against our hypothesis, this is not the case for the chain in which the liquid whey is internally used in cow diets (C chain). We explain this result in the light of the environmental burden of the milk production phase that enter in each chains, irrespective of the followed diet, notwithstanding the average milk yield in group C was not statis- tically lower than those in groups A and B. On this regard, the strategy of grouping cows according to their lactation stage (i.e. beginning and late lactation), and of feeding them different diets (i.e. considering different voluntary feed intake and nutrient re- quirements), usually adopted in large dairy farms, should be tested also in small dairy farms like the one here considered. This strategy could be useful in increasing milk yield, improving the health status of animals while getting possible positive environmental impacts of the primary production in the traditional dairy chain.

Taken together, results obtained in this study were in line with other LCA studies where different dairy products were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Despite different environmental assessment methods used in literature, the milk production, i.e. the main raw material in the mozzarella production, is the process which mostly contributed to the environmental impact in all the categories. In particular, the most impactful categories, coming from normalization results, were the AC, EC and GWP impacts (see Fig. 6). Berlin (2002) showed that the raw milk production is by far the largest contributor to AC, EU, GWP and PO. The main hotspots for AC, EC and GWP environmental impacts were pointed out by Hospido et al. (2003) and Fantin et al. (2012). Hogaas Eide (2002) showed that the dairy farm was the main factor in terms of EC, AC (due to the leakage of nutrients and ammonia losses from manure application) and GWP (caused by the methane emissions from the cattle). Methane from enteric fermentation was the pre- dominant emission source also in Kim et al. (2013) study. Moreover, in Kim et al. (2013) the Climate Change was also impacted by the manure management that caused environmental burden on both GWP and EU categories. Even in van Middelaar et al. (2011) study, the enteric fermentation was the main emission source impacting on GWP category.
Our results and current literature showed that GWP, PO, AC and EU are important categories on which is worth focusing improve- ment alternatives.
The proposal of improvement alternatives at dairy-farm level should be interesting from an environmental point of view in the whole dairy supply chain.

According to Patra (2012), the emission of enteric fermentation might be influenced by the proportion of forages and by the quality of forages in the ration. If it is true that methane emission from the rumen activity is positively related to the portion of roughage in the diet (Kristensen et al., 2011), the negative effect on ruminal health of an excess of non structural carbohydrates (starch and sugars) and the related risk of acidosis should be also considered. Moreover, according to Thomassen et al. (2008), the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the animal slow the rate of fermentation in the rumen. Hospido et al. (2003) and Castanheira et al. (2010) proposed alternatives focused on changes in the dairy ration and on the manure management. According to Kristensen et al. (2015) different types of hay in cow ration do not lead to a significant reduction of GHG emissions.

Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.
18: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
