Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.
20: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. This method considers the following environmental impacts: abiotic depletion (AD), abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (ADf), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), human toxicity (HT), fresh water and marine aquatic eco-toxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial eco-toxicity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), acidifi- cation (AC), and eutrophication (EU). Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

Results on the environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella cheese showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain, irrespective of the diet followed at the case farm level (Diet A, Fig. 2; Diet B, Fig. 3; Diet C, Fig. 4).

This high contribution of milk production phase to the envi- ronmental impact of the dairy chain is in agreement with other studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
Mozzarella production had some impacts on HT, ADf, AD, ME and ODP impacts. The impacts on HT, AD and ME categories were due to the Chromium (on HT and AD categories) and to Nickel (on

Fig. 2. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet A) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet B) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet C) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the case farm.

ME category) from machinery and tools used in milk reception and pasteurization steps. Moreover, cheese factory (i.e. mozzarella production process) had significant thermal energy consumption obtained from the combustion of fossil fuels to produce steam and hot water used in the pasteurisation step. The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a).

Impacts of transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant or from factory to the dairy farm-seemed not very important and this is consistent with the short distance between the locations of the firms involved -the farm and the factory-.

The relative burden of the wastewater treatment (in A and B diet) along the whole dairy chain was negligible and involved mainly the two categories of marine ecotoxicity and eutrophica- tion. Even in this case, our findings appear in line with Gonzalez- Garcia et al. (2013a).

The characterization results (Fig. 5), based on a cradle-to- processing-gate boundary, showed that dairy supply chain in case of A diet had the worst environmental performance on many impact categories. The worst performances of this chain compared

Fig. 5. Results of the characterization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (phys- ical allocation). For each category, the percentage expresses the relative impact of each chain respect to the most impactful dairy supply chain (100%).

to others are on AD, ME, ODP, HT and FW impacts. Even on those impacts where the A chain was not the worst option, lower per- formances than B chain were observed. The A diet performances are mainly related to agricultural processes for hay production, to the wastewater whey disposal, finally, to the milk yield.

On GWP, PO, AC and EU impact categories the differences among the three chains were not so strong, but the dairy chain C appeared the worst solution as mainly related to manure/slurry production in liquid whey recycling diet. On the other side, the C chain was preferable for its lowest impact on AD, ODP and ADf categories, because of the whey usage instead of wastewater disposal, because of agricultural processes for silage production, finally due to milk yield.
The dairy chain with B diet resulted the lowest impacting chain for several categories, even if quite similar impacts to that of the C chain emerged in many cases.

These findings were confirmed when looking at the normali- zation figure (Fig. 6). Even if normalization only scales some impact categories up or down, the magnitude of results was reported for impact categories and each dairy chains. Four impact categories emerged for their magnitude, i.e. acidification, eutrophication, marine ecotoxicity and global warming potential. The impact of A diet was really high compared to the other diets on marine eco- toxicity and, even if to a low degree, also on abiotic and ozone depletion.

The impacts on GWP and PO categories (Table 5) were mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation, confirming current literature (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b). The burden on AC category was mainly due to ammonia emissions from the volatilization of the nitrogen contained in manure, in agreement with Berlin (2002) study.

With regard to the EU impact, the main eutrophying emissions that contribute to the EU category were the ammonia emissions and the leaching of nitrate derived from the manure application on the field (Table these results were in agreement with Battini et al. (2016) and Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013b) studies.

Impacts on AD category were due to Copper (chain A), Lead (chain B) and Chromium (chain C) coming from agricultural pro- cesses for fodder production, which are different among chains, and milk reception and pasteurization steps.

The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a). Moreover, the transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant in A and B chains, or from factory to the dairy farm in C chain-, notwithstanding the short distance between the locations involved, impacted on AD, ADf, and ODP categories.
The impact on ME was due to Beryllium coming from the disposal treatment of the cheese whey in WWTP (A and B chains). Moreover, Copper and Zinc due to manure/slurry spreading caused
Fig. 6. Results of the normalization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (physical allocation).

Impact categories|Dairy chains|Substances|Processes AD|A|Copper|Agricultural processes for fodder production, milk reception and pasteurization steps |B|Lead| |C|Chromium| ADf|A, B. C|Oil crude|Pasteurisation GWP|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation ODP|A, B. C|Methane bromotrifluoro, Halon 1301|Oil combustion and extraction during pasteurization step HT|A, B. C|Chromium|Milk reception and pasteurization steps FW|A, B. C|Copper|Manure/slurry spreading ME|A, B|Beryllium|WWTP TE|A, B. C|Zinc and Copper|Manure/slurry spreading PO|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation AC|A, B. C|Ammonia|Manure/slurry storage and spreading EU|A, B. C|Ammonia and nitrate|Manure/slurry storage and spreading

impacts on FW and TE categories. According to these findings, as expected, we cannot find a dairy chain to be considered the best option under a global perspective across all impact categories. Moreover and against our hypothesis, this is not the case for the chain in which the liquid whey is internally used in cow diets (C chain). We explain this result in the light of the environmental burden of the milk production phase that enter in each chains, irrespective of the followed diet, notwithstanding the average milk yield in group C was not statis- tically lower than those in groups A and B. On this regard, the strategy of grouping cows according to their lactation stage (i.e. beginning and late lactation), and of feeding them different diets (i.e. considering different voluntary feed intake and nutrient re- quirements), usually adopted in large dairy farms, should be tested also in small dairy farms like the one here considered. This strategy could be useful in increasing milk yield, improving the health status of animals while getting possible positive environmental impacts of the primary production in the traditional dairy chain.

Taken together, results obtained in this study were in line with other LCA studies where different dairy products were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Despite different environmental assessment methods used in literature, the milk production, i.e. the main raw material in the mozzarella production, is the process which mostly contributed to the environmental impact in all the categories. In particular, the most impactful categories, coming from normalization results, were the AC, EC and GWP impacts (see Fig. 6). Berlin (2002) showed that the raw milk production is by far the largest contributor to AC, EU, GWP and PO. The main hotspots for AC, EC and GWP environmental impacts were pointed out by Hospido et al. (2003) and Fantin et al. (2012). Hogaas Eide (2002) showed that the dairy farm was the main factor in terms of EC, AC (due to the leakage of nutrients and ammonia losses from manure application) and GWP (caused by the methane emissions from the cattle). Methane from enteric fermentation was the pre- dominant emission source also in Kim et al. (2013) study. Moreover, in Kim et al. (2013) the Climate Change was also impacted by the manure management that caused environmental burden on both GWP and EU categories. Even in van Middelaar et al. (2011) study, the enteric fermentation was the main emission source impacting on GWP category.
Our results and current literature showed that GWP, PO, AC and EU are important categories on which is worth focusing improve- ment alternatives.
The proposal of improvement alternatives at dairy-farm level should be interesting from an environmental point of view in the whole dairy supply chain.

According to Patra (2012), the emission of enteric fermentation might be influenced by the proportion of forages and by the quality of forages in the ration. If it is true that methane emission from the rumen activity is positively related to the portion of roughage in the diet (Kristensen et al., 2011), the negative effect on ruminal health of an excess of non structural carbohydrates (starch and sugars) and the related risk of acidosis should be also considered. Moreover, according to Thomassen et al. (2008), the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the animal slow the rate of fermentation in the rumen. Hospido et al. (2003) and Castanheira et al. (2010) proposed alternatives focused on changes in the dairy ration and on the manure management. According to Kristensen et al. (2015) different types of hay in cow ration do not lead to a significant reduction of GHG emissions.

Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.

This strategy could be useful in increasing milk yield, improving the health status of animals while getting possible positive environmental impacts of the primary production in the traditional dairy chain.

Taken together, results obtained in this study were in line with other LCA studies where different dairy products were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Despite different environmental assessment methods used in literature, the milk production, i.e. the main raw material in the mozzarella production, is the process which mostly contributed to the environmental impact in all the categories. In particular, the most impactful categories, coming from normalization results, were the AC, EC and GWP impacts (see Fig. 6). Berlin (2002) showed that the raw milk production is by far the largest contributor to AC, EU, GWP and PO. The main hotspots for AC, EC and GWP environmental impacts were pointed out by Hospido et al. (2003) and Fantin et al. (2012). Hogaas Eide (2002) showed that the dairy farm was the main factor in terms of EC, AC (due to the leakage of nutrients and ammonia losses from manure application) and GWP (caused by the methane emissions from the cattle). Methane from enteric fermentation was the pre- dominant emission source also in Kim et al. (2013) study. Moreover, in Kim et al. (2013) the Climate Change was also impacted by the manure management that caused environmental burden on both GWP and EU categories. Even in van Middelaar et al. (2011) study, the enteric fermentation was the main emission source impacting on GWP category.
Our results and current literature showed that GWP, PO, AC and EU are important categories on which is worth focusing improve- ment alternatives.
The proposal of improvement alternatives at dairy-farm level should be interesting from an environmental point of view in the whole dairy supply chain.

According to Patra (2012), the emission of enteric fermentation might be influenced by the proportion of forages and by the quality of forages in the ration. If it is true that methane emission from the rumen activity is positively related to the portion of roughage in the diet (Kristensen et al., 2011), the negative effect on ruminal health of an excess of non structural carbohydrates (starch and sugars) and the related risk of acidosis should be also considered. Moreover, according to Thomassen et al. (2008), the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the animal slow the rate of fermentation in the rumen. Hospido et al. (2003) and Castanheira et al. (2010) proposed alternatives focused on changes in the dairy ration and on the manure management. According to Kristensen et al. (2015) different types of hay in cow ration do not lead to a significant reduction of GHG emissions.

Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.
23: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. This method considers the following environmental impacts: abiotic depletion (AD), abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (ADf), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), human toxicity (HT), fresh water and marine aquatic eco-toxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial eco-toxicity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), acidifi- cation (AC), and eutrophication (EU). Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

Results on the environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella cheese showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain, irrespective of the diet followed at the case farm level (Diet A, Fig. 2; Diet B, Fig. 3; Diet C, Fig. 4).

This high contribution of milk production phase to the envi- ronmental impact of the dairy chain is in agreement with other studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
Mozzarella production had some impacts on HT, ADf, AD, ME and ODP impacts. The impacts on HT, AD and ME categories were due to the Chromium (on HT and AD categories) and to Nickel (on

Fig. 2. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet A) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet B) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet C) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the case farm.

ME category) from machinery and tools used in milk reception and pasteurization steps. Moreover, cheese factory (i.e. mozzarella production process) had significant thermal energy consumption obtained from the combustion of fossil fuels to produce steam and hot water used in the pasteurisation step. The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a).

Impacts of transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant or from factory to the dairy farm-seemed not very important and this is consistent with the short distance between the locations of the firms involved -the farm and the factory-.

The relative burden of the wastewater treatment (in A and B diet) along the whole dairy chain was negligible and involved mainly the two categories of marine ecotoxicity and eutrophica- tion. Even in this case, our findings appear in line with Gonzalez- Garcia et al. (2013a).

The characterization results (Fig. 5), based on a cradle-to- processing-gate boundary, showed that dairy supply chain in case of A diet had the worst environmental performance on many impact categories. The worst performances of this chain compared

Fig. 5. Results of the characterization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (phys- ical allocation). For each category, the percentage expresses the relative impact of each chain respect to the most impactful dairy supply chain (100%).

to others are on AD, ME, ODP, HT and FW impacts. Even on those impacts where the A chain was not the worst option, lower per- formances than B chain were observed. The A diet performances are mainly related to agricultural processes for hay production, to the wastewater whey disposal, finally, to the milk yield.

On GWP, PO, AC and EU impact categories the differences among the three chains were not so strong, but the dairy chain C appeared the worst solution as mainly related to manure/slurry production in liquid whey recycling diet. On the other side, the C chain was preferable for its lowest impact on AD, ODP and ADf categories, because of the whey usage instead of wastewater disposal, because of agricultural processes for silage production, finally due to milk yield.
The dairy chain with B diet resulted the lowest impacting chain for several categories, even if quite similar impacts to that of the C chain emerged in many cases.

These findings were confirmed when looking at the normali- zation figure (Fig. 6). Even if normalization only scales some impact categories up or down, the magnitude of results was reported for impact categories and each dairy chains. Four impact categories emerged for their magnitude, i.e. acidification, eutrophication, marine ecotoxicity and global warming potential. The impact of A diet was really high compared to the other diets on marine eco- toxicity and, even if to a low degree, also on abiotic and ozone depletion.

The impacts on GWP and PO categories (Table 5) were mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation, confirming current literature (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b). (2016) and Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013b) studies.

The impact on ME was due to Beryllium coming from the disposal treatment of the cheese whey in WWTP (A and B chains). Moreover, Copper and Zinc due to manure/slurry spreading caused

Impacts on AD category were due to Copper (chain A), Lead (chain B) and Chromium (chain C) coming from agricultural pro- cesses for fodder production, which are different among chains, and milk reception and pasteurization steps.

The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a). Moreover, the transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant in A and B chains, or from factory to the dairy farm in C chain-, notwithstanding the short distance between the locations involved, impacted on AD, ADf, and ODP categories.
The impact on ME was due to Beryllium coming from the disposal treatment of the cheese whey in WWTP (A and B chains). Moreover, Copper and Zinc due to manure/slurry spreading caused
Fig. 6. Results of the normalization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (physical allocation).
Table 5 Results of characterization (baseline allocation): the most impactful processes and substances for dairy supply chain and for impact category.

Impact categories|Dairy chains|Substances|Processes AD|A|Copper|Agricultural processes for fodder production, milk reception and pasteurization steps |B|Lead| |C|Chromium| ADf|A, B. C|Oil crude|Pasteurisation GWP|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation ODP|A, B. C|Methane bromotrifluoro, Halon 1301|Oil combustion and extraction during pasteurization step HT|A, B. C|Chromium|Milk reception and pasteurization steps FW|A, B. C|Copper|Manure/slurry spreading ME|A, B|Beryllium|WWTP TE|A, B. C|Zinc and Copper|Manure/slurry spreading PO|A, B. C|Methane biogenic|Enteric fermentation AC|A, B. C|Ammonia|Manure/slurry storage and spreading EU|A, B. C|Ammonia and nitrate|Manure/slurry storage and spreading

impacts on FW and TE categories. According to these findings, as expected, we cannot find a dairy chain to be considered the best option under a global perspective across all impact categories. Moreover and against our hypothesis, this is not the case for the chain in which the liquid whey is internally used in cow diets (C chain). We explain this result in the light of the environmental burden of the milk production phase that enter in each chains, irrespective of the followed diet, notwithstanding the average milk yield in group C was not statis- tically lower than those in groups A and B. On this regard, the strategy of grouping cows according to their lactation stage (i.e. beginning and late lactation), and of feeding them different diets (i.e. considering different voluntary feed intake and nutrient re- quirements), usually adopted in large dairy farms, should be tested also in small dairy farms like the one here considered. This strategy could be useful in increasing milk yield, improving the health status of animals while getting possible positive environmental impacts of the primary production in the traditional dairy chain.

Taken together, results obtained in this study were in line with other LCA studies where different dairy products were assessed from an environmental point of view (Berlin, 2002; Fantin et al., 2012; Hogaas Eide, 2002; Hospido et al., 2003; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). Despite different environmental assessment methods used in literature, the milk production, i.e. the main raw material in the mozzarella production, is the process which mostly contributed to the environmental impact in all the categories. In particular, the most impactful categories, coming from normalization results, were the AC, EC and GWP impacts (see Fig. 6). Berlin (2002) showed that the raw milk production is by far the largest contributor to AC, EU, GWP and PO. The main hotspots for AC, EC and GWP environmental impacts were pointed out by Hospido et al. (2003) and Fantin et al. (2012). Hogaas Eide (2002) showed that the dairy farm was the main factor in terms of EC, AC (due to the leakage of nutrients and ammonia losses from manure application) and GWP (caused by the methane emissions from the cattle). Methane from enteric fermentation was the pre- dominant emission source also in Kim et al. (2013) study. Moreover, in Kim et al. (2013) the Climate Change was also impacted by the manure management that caused environmental burden on both GWP and EU categories. Even in van Middelaar et al. (2011) study, the enteric fermentation was the main emission source impacting on GWP category.
Our results and current literature showed that GWP, PO, AC and EU are important categories on which is worth focusing improve- ment alternatives.
The proposal of improvement alternatives at dairy-farm level should be interesting from an environmental point of view in the whole dairy supply chain.

According to Patra (2012), the emission of enteric fermentation might be influenced by the proportion of forages and by the quality of forages in the ration. If it is true that methane emission from the rumen activity is positively related to the portion of roughage in the diet (Kristensen et al., 2011), the negative effect on ruminal health of an excess of non structural carbohydrates (starch and sugars) and the related risk of acidosis should be also considered. Moreover, according to Thomassen et al. (2008), the high intake of roughage and the low content of starch in the animal slow the rate of fermentation in the rumen. Hospido et al. (2003) and Castanheira et al. (2010) proposed alternatives focused on changes in the dairy ration and on the manure management. According to Kristensen et al. (2015) different types of hay in cow ration do not lead to a significant reduction of GHG emissions.

Even the farm regime could cause several differences in the environmental impact of milk production; in fact, some Authors (Thomassen et al., 2008; van der Werf et al., 2009) showed that organic milk production implies higher methane emissions than the conventional process due to the lower milk production per cow and to the increased use of roughage in the ration.

Improvements in the environmental profile of cheese produc- tion should be also focused at dairy factory level which requires a high energy consumption for machineries used during the pro- duction process.
26: 3.1. Environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella: baseline allocation
In order to assess the environmental impact of 123 g of

mozzarella cheese, the CML- IA baseline (v. 3.01/EU 25) method was used. This method considers the following environmental impacts: abiotic depletion (AD), abiotic depletion (fossil fuels) (ADf), global warming (GWP), ozone layer depletion (ODP), human toxicity (HT), fresh water and marine aquatic eco-toxicity (FW, ME), terrestrial eco-toxicity (TE), photochemical oxidation (PO), acidifi- cation (AC), and eutrophication (EU). Characterization and normalization, based on Europe region, were carried out to identify both the most impactful phase along the dairy chain and the most impactful diet.

Results on the environmental impact of 123 g of mozzarella cheese showed that raw milk production was the most impactful phase along the considered supply chain, irrespective of the diet followed at the case farm level (Diet A, Fig. 2; Diet B, Fig. 3; Diet C, Fig. 4).

This high contribution of milk production phase to the envi- ronmental impact of the dairy chain is in agreement with other studies (Fantin et al., 2012; Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b; Kim et al., 2013; van Middelaar et al., 2011). A remarkable contri- bution of the dairy farm in categories such as AC, EU and GWP was assessed, in line with the above studies. This result was due to methane emission from the enteric fermentation process (on GWP impact), and to ammonia emissions due to manure management and spreading (on AC and EU impacts).
Mozzarella production had some impacts on HT, ADf, AD, ME and ODP impacts. The impacts on HT, AD and ME categories were due to the Chromium (on HT and AD categories) and to Nickel (on

Fig. 2. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet A) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 3. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet B) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the wastewater treatment plant (WWTP).

Fig. 4. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella cheese: milk (Diet C) and mozzarella production (physical allocation). Transport refers both to the milk and to the liquid whey, delivered respectively to the dairy factory and to the case farm.

ME category) from machinery and tools used in milk reception and pasteurization steps. Moreover, cheese factory (i.e. mozzarella production process) had significant thermal energy consumption obtained from the combustion of fossil fuels to produce steam and hot water used in the pasteurisation step. The extraction of fuel oil and its combustion in the boiler are contributors to categories such as ADf and ODP, confirming results of Gonzalez-Garcia et al. (2013a).

Impacts of transportation of milk -from dairy farm to cheese factory- and of whey -either from factory to the wastewater plant or from factory to the dairy farm-seemed not very important and this is consistent with the short distance between the locations of the firms involved -the farm and the factory-.

The relative burden of the wastewater treatment (in A and B diet) along the whole dairy chain was negligible and involved mainly the two categories of marine ecotoxicity and eutrophica- tion. Even in this case, our findings appear in line with Gonzalez- Garcia et al. (2013a).

The characterization results (Fig. 5), based on a cradle-to- processing-gate boundary, showed that dairy supply chain in case of A diet had the worst environmental performance on many impact categories. The worst performances of this chain compared

Fig. 5. Results of the characterization. Comparison among dairy supply chains (phys- ical allocation). For each category, the percentage expresses the relative impact of each chain respect to the most impactful dairy supply chain (100%).

to others are on AD, ME, ODP, HT and FW impacts. Even on those impacts where the A chain was not the worst option, lower per- formances than B chain were observed. The A diet performances are mainly related to agricultural processes for hay production, to the wastewater whey disposal, finally, to the milk yield.

On GWP, PO, AC and EU impact categories the differences among the three chains were not so strong, but the dairy chain C appeared the worst solution as mainly related to manure/slurry production in liquid whey recycling diet. On the other side, the C chain was preferable for its lowest impact on AD, ODP and ADf categories, because of the whey usage instead of wastewater disposal, because of agricultural processes for silage production, finally due to milk yield.
The dairy chain with B diet resulted the lowest impacting chain for several categories, even if quite similar impacts to that of the C chain emerged in many cases.

These findings were confirmed when looking at the normali- zation figure (Fig. 6). Even if normalization only scales some impact categories up or down, the magnitude of results was reported for impact categories and each dairy chains. Four impact categories emerged for their magnitude, i.e. acidification, eutrophication, marine ecotoxicity and global warming potential. The impact of A diet was really high compared to the other diets on marine eco- toxicity and, even if to a low degree, also on abiotic and ozone depletion.

The impacts on GWP and PO categories (Table 5) were mainly due to methane emissions derived from the enteric fermentation, confirming current literature (Gonzalez-Garcia et al., 2013a, 2013b). The burden on AC category was mainly due to ammonia emissions from the volatilization of the nitrogen contained in manure, in agreement with Berlin (2002) study.

27: A first sensitivity analysis was aimed to test results against an allocation method (CU) different from the one applied in the baseline analysis (physical allocation).

The sensitivity analysis showed that results from the physical allocation were higher than results achieved through a CU alloca- tion for each dairy supply chains (Figs. 7-9). Moreover, comparing findings based on CU allocation among dairy supply chains gave
Fig. 7. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella: dairy supply chain A (physical versus CU allocation).
Fig. 8. Environmental impacts of 123 of mozzarella: dairy supply chain B (Physical versus CU allocation).
Fig. 9. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella: dairy supply chain C (Physical versus CU allocation).
results in line with those presented in Figs. 5-6 following the physical allocation method (for this reason, data were not reported).

Again, the problem of assessing the chain with the best envi- ronmental performance had no a unique solution. The A chain had the worst performances under some impact categories, the C chain under other categories. The B chain resulted the least impacting for eight out of eleven impact categories, even if slight differences among chains emerged in many cases.

According to Flysjo et al. (2014), when whey is used for feeding animals, the environmental impacts due to animal feed avoided, e.g. an alternative carbohydrate source of feed like barley, should be subtracted from the total emissions of considered system. In our study case, liquid whey was offered to cows in partial substitution of drinking water; however, we also simulated the exclusion of barley from the C diet and performed a new analysis, but results concerning both the most impactful phase and chain were un- changed (data are available on request).

A further sensitivity analysis was carried out to illustrate what the effect would have been considering the variability of milk yield (converted in FPCM) observed in the three diets. New results related to the minimum and maximum values of milk diet for each diet were compared to the average yield considered in the baseline analysis (detailed results are available on request).

At a global perspective, the environmental impacts of the diets changed according to the following pattern: A diet -3.2% at maximum yield, +6.6% at minimum yield; B diet -4.7% at maximum yield, +3.2% at minimum yield; C diet -2.5% at maximum yield, +2.6% at minimum yield). The highest variation, both in negative and positive signs, involved the categories of acidification (AC) and eutrophication (EU).

Confirming the baseline results, A diet still had the highest impacts in case the milk yield reaches its maximum value, and even more at the minimum. In the same way, B diet at maximum yield (27.64 L/d) represented the first best solution over all impact cat- egories but ozone layer depletion; for this category, the C diet was the best option at maximum milk production level (26.51 L/d). Moreover, the above C diet resulted the second best on several impacts, namely AD, ADf, HT, FW, ME and TE.

Drivers for these impact categories were obviously those dis- cussed in the baseline scenario. Focusing on C diet at maximum production level and comparing the new findings with those over all diets at the average yield, the sensitivity analysis produced some interesting results. The C diet at the highest yield had the lowest environmental impact over all categories and diets. The C diet at the average yield (25.78 L/d) was better than the B diet at the minimum production value (25.26 L/d). Finally, the C diet at the minimum yield (25.05 L/d) is even less impacting than the B diet at the minimum production value on AD, ADf and ODP impact categories.

Summarizing these findings, results obtained in the baseline diets and, most of all, the conclusion about the performance of C diet should be carefully considered. As the sensitivity analysis re- ported, testing for the variability of milk yield may change previous results in a way that the C diet with whey usage could get a very good environmental performance.

31: A first sensitivity analysis was aimed to test results against an allocation method (CU) different from the one applied in the baseline analysis (physical allocation).

The sensitivity analysis showed that results from the physical allocation were higher than results achieved through a CU alloca- tion for each dairy supply chains (Figs. 7-9). Moreover, comparing findings based on CU allocation among dairy supply chains gave
Fig. 7. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella: dairy supply chain A (physical versus CU allocation).
Fig. 8. Environmental impacts of 123 of mozzarella: dairy supply chain B (Physical versus CU allocation).
Fig. 9. Environmental impacts of 123 g of mozzarella: dairy supply chain C (Physical versus CU allocation).
results in line with those presented in Figs. 5-6 following the physical allocation method (for this reason, data were not reported).

Again, the problem of assessing the chain with the best envi- ronmental performance had no a unique solution. The A chain had the worst performances under some impact categories, the C chain under other categories. The B chain resulted the least impacting for eight out of eleven impact categories, even if slight differences among chains emerged in many cases.

According to Flysjo et al. (2014), when whey is used for feeding animals, the environmental impacts due to animal feed avoided, e.g. an alternative carbohydrate source of feed like barley, should be subtracted from the total emissions of considered system. In our study case, liquid whey was offered to cows in partial substitution of drinking water; however, we also simulated the exclusion of barley from the C diet and performed a new analysis, but results concerning both the most impactful phase and chain were un- changed (data are available on request).

A further sensitivity analysis was carried out to illustrate what the effect would have been considering the variability of milk yield (converted in FPCM) observed in the three diets. New results related to the minimum and maximum values of milk diet for each diet were compared to the average yield considered in the baseline analysis (detailed results are available on request).

At a global perspective, the environmental impacts of the diets changed according to the following pattern: A diet -3.2% at maximum yield, +6.6% at minimum yield; B diet -4.7% at maximum yield, +3.2% at minimum yield; C diet -2.5% at maximum yield, +2.6% at minimum yield). The highest variation, both in negative and positive signs, involved the categories of acidification (AC) and eutrophication (EU).

Confirming the baseline results, A diet still had the highest impacts in case the milk yield reaches its maximum value, and even more at the minimum. In the same way, B diet at maximum yield (27.64 L/d) represented the first best solution over all impact cat- egories but ozone layer depletion; for this category, the C diet was the best option at maximum milk production level (26.51 L/d). Moreover, the above C diet resulted the second best on several impacts, namely AD, ADf, HT, FW, ME and TE.

Drivers for these impact categories were obviously those dis- cussed in the baseline scenario. Focusing on C diet at maximum production level and comparing the new findings with those over all diets at the average yield, the sensitivity analysis produced some interesting results. The C diet at the highest yield had the lowest environmental impact over all categories and diets. The C diet at the average yield (25.78 L/d) was better than the B diet at the minimum production value (25.26 L/d). Finally, the C diet at the minimum yield (25.05 L/d) is even less impacting than the B diet at the minimum production value on AD, ADf and ODP impact categories.

Summarizing these findings, results obtained in the baseline diets and, most of all, the conclusion about the performance of C diet should be carefully considered. As the sensitivity analysis re- ported, testing for the variability of milk yield may change previous results in a way that the C diet with whey usage could get a very good environmental performance.
32: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 33: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 34: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 35: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 36: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 37: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 38: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 39: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 40: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 41: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain.
