42: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 43: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 44: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 45: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 46: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 47: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 48: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain. 49: In this paper, the environmental impact of mozzarella cheese production in an Italian case study is assessed following three different diets at farm level and considering the chance of using the liquid whey within the traditional dairy chain.

One of the main findings of the study was that the primary phase, i.e. milk production, had the highest environmental impact within the supply chain, in line with other life cycle studies of dairy products that were discussed in the text.

In order to reduce the environmental impact of milk production and to decrease the wastage of mozzarella cheese-making, we assessed the environmental impact of dairy supply chains (at farm and factory levels) changing the diet composition at case farm level. Moreover, in C diet we introduced microbiologically stabilized liquid whey in balanced dairy cow ration as partial replacement of drinking water. Apart from liquid whey, C diet included silages. In the other two dairy chains, the liquid whey was delivered to a wastewater treatment system.

Our hypothesis was that using the by-product of mozzarella cheese production within the local chain would gain advantages under an environmental perspective, and indirectly under eco- nomic and nutritional viewpoints. However, baseline findings did not completely support our hypothesis. Compared with other dairy chains, the C chain did not result the least impacting solution at a global environmental perspective, i.e. the C chain was the best option for some impacts, but it was not considering other impacts. This finding is mainly due to the high weigh of the raw milk pro- duction that impacted in all dairy chains irrespective of the cow diet. Most of the differences in baseline results are small and, indeed, they are influenced by the assumption that milk yields was not resulted statistically different among diets. However, the sensitivity analysis carried out at minimum and maximum values of intervals of confidence of the mean values showed that liquid whey introduction in dairy cow feeding could contribute to lower the environmental impact of the milk production. These findings sug- gest the need to deepen investigations that allow comprehensive comparisons.

Besides these considerations, improvement alternatives within the dairy farm, even if difficult, would require testing for further feeding and manure management strategies, while pro- environmental changes within the dairy factory should involve energy sources and requirements and in site emissions.

Finally, recycling the liquid whey and strengthening the relation at local level between dairy farms and cheese factories, may have several economic benefits: the cost of whey transportation to treatment plants in charge of dairy factories and the disposal costs
of liquid whey would be eliminated. More importantly, the envi- ronmental burden of whey treatment could be avoided.

<table>The benefits of liquid whey use in dairy cow feeding could be|00003600/3620-rapporto-85-2008-inventario-nazionale-agricoltura-alta.pdf/ view (accessed 27.06.16).
relevant especially in the traditional dairy chain because this practice is simple, cheap and suitable even in small farms.|Istat, 2013. Agriculture and Livestock Database, Italy. http://agri.istat.it/ (accessed 27.06.16).
For these reasons, further studies on liquid whey recycling benefits should be carried out in order to find out win-win stra- tegies for a green innovation of the traditional dairy chain.
